sentences.text: When we arrived to Leninsk, I think it was summer.
And we wanted to see what-- how we're going to survive there.
Because, you know, my father didn't want to go to work in his profession, because they were saying whoever works in Russia has to steal, otherwise he can't survive.
And so he-- he wasn't ready to do that.
So for a while, we didn't do anything.
And-- and then I can't remember distinctly when it happened.
They used to sell something on the black market.
My-- my uncle used to go out and sell this and that, or whatever they could to survive.
And then somebody said that he-- that perhaps he could try to do-- to manufacture soap.
I-- I don't remember exactly how he came about it.
But then one day I remember, the-- early in the morning, the Russian babushkas used to come to our place, and bringing oil and bringing caustic soda.
And I said to Dad, what are you going to do with that?
He said, I'm going to make soap.
So I says, so how do you know how to make soap?
Somebody told me how to do it.
I'm going to try it.
And so the factory started in our house.
And they used to come.
They used to steal, actually, that caustic soda and oil from the factory they used to work, the Russian women.
They used to come at 4 o'clock in the morning.
And they had the balloons of oil and soda attached to their legs, and used the big-- how do you call it-- the skirts, so it covered them.
And nobody-- somehow they-- either they knew the people who watched the factories that they're doing it or not, I'm not sure.
But it was going on for a while.
My father used to make the soap, and Mother used to help him cutting it in pieces, and dry it.
And then my uncle used to go out on the black market and sell it.
This was quite a good thing, business, because--
Well, it was a-- see, they had a-- like a bazaar, like a-- like a-- how do you call it-- markets.
They used to go once or twice a week.
There was-- the people used to come from other country towns.
And they used to buy these sort of produce.
And my uncle used to go around selling it, and we made quite a good living out of it.
It was a little city.
It wasn't a village, actually.
There was villages-- there were villages around Leninsk.
It was a little city.
I don't know how many inhabitants there were, perhaps 10,000, you know.
But they had a school.
They had a gymnasium.
It's like a municipality, and so on and so on.
And we children went to school, of course.
There were lots-- there were lots of Jews who escaped from Ukraine.
Because the war started and-- and thanks for that, they survived.
Because if they would have stayed in the Ukraine, they would never survive.
And my father made lots of friends with people from Kiev, and I had a few girlfriends, which, you know, we became very friendly with their families.
Yeah, well sort of.
But mostly it was around the school we used to make different programs in the school, and the parents used to come there.
But there wasn't really a communal, per se.
But people we used to meet in each other's homes and talk, and--
No, no, no, no.
It was a Russian school.
I wouldn't know.
I really wouldn't know.
I really couldn't tell you that.
But mostly who were there, they were people who run away from Ukraine, or from Moscow, or from whatever, Kharkov, [?
I had a girl ?] from Kharkov, from Kiev.
They were living, thanks-- thanks to that, they survived the war.
Otherwise, they would have never survived.
No, not at all, except at home, of course.
We used to-- the holidays or whatever, Shabbat we used to make.
But no Jewish education, no.
But I used to love school.
I always loved school.
I really was good at school, and I loved going to school.
Because it was-- it breaks the monotony of the worries of-- but it wasn't easy.
And we actually it was time when really we used to stay in the queues for bread.
It wasn't much bread at home.
And I remember I used-- they would-- they used to send out, to stay, and then we used to change with Father or Mother in staying in the queue.
And-- and I remember mother used to divide this bread for six pieces.
Everybody sort of guard their own piece of bread.
If you would eaten it up in one go, you wouldn't have to eat all day.
It wasn't easy.
I mean, if I tell this story, my kids maybe not-- won't believe it that something like that happened.
But it did happen, you know.

And I can't sort of visualize if I could do that now to my children, to divide a piece of bread, whatever I was given from the government, and to be exact to divide for six pieces, and everybody guard their own piece of bread.
Because if I would eaten up my sister's bread, she wouldn't have it to eat.
So it wasn't-- it's very hard to understand that as long as you, when you go through that.
But I still remember, in fact once, we were told there's-- they were giving soup for-- for-- for families of the elite of the soldiers, from-- who were fighting the war.
They've got a special cuisine, a special kitchen, which you can go there and get some food to take home.
And I remember I went.
My Russian was perfect.
I could go away for a Russian girl like I would have been born there.
And I was telling lies.
They was, from whom have you got at the war-- at the front?
I said my-- my brother and my daddy is there.
And you see, they wouldn't believe.
They used to bring a half a bucket of food-- of-- of soup.
And Mom used to put a bit of flour in, and so that we could eat the whole day.
It's-- I can't even believe that that ever happened.
But it did happen.
And it happened.

I used to go out at nighttime, 3 o'clock in the morning, or 2 o'clock in the morning, staying the whole day, the whole morning, till 6 o'clock in the morning to get, say, a loaf of bread for the family, and bringing it home.
Then mom used to divide it for everybody, the same piece.
God forbid, some-- you know, one child or-- or them get a little bit less.
It was-- it was terrible.
Yeah, we had ration cards, yes.
We were, actually we were given cards, so much you can have, no more.
And every day we used to go and stand in-- in queues to get food.
They got it from the government.
So--
Uzbeks, mainly were Uzbeks, and there were Russians as well.
But the Russians mostly were Jews who run away.
There were not many inhabitants, Russian inhabitants in Uzbekistan.
They mostly were Uzbeks.
No, no I wouldn't say that, no.
Because I mean I've got some photographs of friends of mine, which also run away.
Some of them run away also from home.
They were Russians, and we were very good friends.
And never nobody told me dirty Jew or something like that.
I never had that problem at all.
No.
We didn't know a thing about the war, not a thing, until the liberation, when I had an uncle, my-- my-- my mother's brother, who was in the army from Russia, went into Poland and came into Lublin.
And he-- he wrote a letter to my mom.
That was in maybe the beginning of '45 or-- or just on '45 that he came to Lublin.
And nobody-- and we heard about it.
We never knew or thing what was going on in Poland, not a thing.
He wrote to my mother, and I remember mom crying.
Because she couldn't believe-- we could not believe it that anything like that happened in Poland.
Because everybody perished, grandfather and the children, and their family, and the sisters.
No, nobody was left, only later on-- my father found a cousin.
No.
No, nobody knew anything, not at all.
We did not knew a thing till almost the end of the war, what's-- what's going on in Poland.
The-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] the Auschwitz or Majdanek, nobody know anything about it, not a thing.
They must have either covered it up, I don't-- I mean Russia was at war with Germany.
And I can't understand why we did not know it.
But-- we never-- never knew a word, not one word about it.
We heard.
A matter of fact, we were-- we-- we heard about the Russian army goes into Germany, and they used to send-- they used to send parcels to their families, you know, from Germany.
And I remember, like today, we went to some performance at school.
And I-- and I was singing, a cabaret singer, and I needed a long dress.
So I went to a friend of mine.
She just got a parcel from Germany, a beautiful white long dress, and she lent it to me.
And that was sent from her brother or somebody from Russia.
So that's all we know that the German-- that the Russian army is going further into Russia toward Germany.
But otherwise we didn't knew a thing, not a thing.
Maybe-- maybe my father did.
But I don't think that we children knew anything about it.
We never talked about it.
And then what--
I can't understand why-- just now it's coming back to me.
I said, I-- I really can't understand why we didn't-- we never talked about it at all.
And then what happened, when the war finished, which was in May '45, my father start dreaming, he wants to go back to Poland.
And-- and how can we do it?
There was no possibilities.
I had a friend, which her mother was a doctor.
They also came, run away from Kiev.
And she was, you know, the doctors got always something to do with the hierarchy of the Russian, or from the militia and this.
And somehow we managed through her, with her help, we managed to get documents to go sort of for a holiday to Lvov, the whole family.
And we managed to go away at the end of '45.
And no Jew was yet-- no-- no Polish Jew was allowed to leave Russia.
I remember the whole school came to say goodbye to us, all my friends, and everybody was crying.
And we were-- we were happy.
I-- I wasn't very happy.
Because I left all my friends, and I wanted to finish school, which I didn't.
And-- and we arrived in Moscow.
And my father had an uncle there who stayed back, before the First World War.
We stayed with them for a week.
And then we managed to get over to-- also semi-legally to-- Lvov, and from Lvov we came over to Cracow.
We stayed in Cracow from 1946 through '47, for almost nine months, nearly a year.
Yeah.
We were all together in Cracow.
[SIGHS] I was 20, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] or 18 or whatever.
I can't remember exactly.
And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] then it was the Kielce Pogrom.
You must have heard about it.
And my father said to us, I don't want my children to stay anymore in Poland.
They still had to stay back, because he had to liquidate, whatever he had to do.
And we were staying-- when we-- when we were in Poland we stayed in a youth kibbutz, all three children--
In-- in--
In Cracow.
We didn't stay with my parents.
My parents were living on their own.
And we stayed in a youth kibbutz.
Was why I was that?
Because it was a Zionist kibbutz.
We were dreaming.
We were thinking about going eventually to Israel.
And-- and the conditions was also good.
Because we-- you know there was food, and everything was there.
We got Jewish education, and Hebrew, and just-- you know.
It wasn't actually a kibbutz.
I-- I-- I would say it was more so-- what can I tell you-- a house.
It was housing is-- all the-- I think children from-- 12 to about 20, 20 years old, kids.
And it was a Zionist organization, right.
And then when the Kielce Pogrom was-- my father decided we are not staying anymore in Poland.
He wants to send-- and the kibbutz decided to liquidate.
And-- and the whole group of us--
Oh, maybe a hundred.
We went over to Austria.
And-- in at a misplaced-- a displaced person also-- how do you call it-- a-- a misplaced persons camp.
A DP camp, which was Wegscheid, it was Wegscheid.
It's next to Linz in Austria.
And we stayed there for a few months, again.
Every-- every-- every group like the Zionist groups were [NON-ENGLISH].
And there was-- Dror.
It was six or seven different associations of the Zionist organizations-- every-- and every group had its own barrack.
Youth groups, right.
And from then--
Oh, maybe nine months.
Oh, what should I tell you?
Yes, I applied for a job-- not applied-- I heard about this job vacancy at the boss-- at the DP camps, which they-- they need somebody who speaks English.
And my English wasn't very good.
It's not good still now.
But my parents, when we came to Cracow I had a few private lessons.
So I could communicate.
And I was working as a secretary in the camp, which I loved.
Because people was coming in, and you know, this was something.
I was helping a lot of people.
Because the Joint was sending clothes.
We didn't have much.
So, I used to help them to go to collect and to-- it wasn't new clothing-- but everybody-- so I felt important, put it this way.
No, well for-- for the-- I don't know how many.
It was maybe a couple of thousand people there living, mostly young.
Later, my parents arrived from Cracow.
And they-- they allocated also a-- a room in one of the barracks, was also for the parents.
So they were staying with us for a while.
And then I met my husband there.
Somebody introduced us.
And somehow it clicked.
I was going out, I had some other boyfriends.
But somehow when I met him, I decided that may be the one.
And we illegally decided to go over to-- to-- to-- to Italy.
My parents, they still stayed back in-- in the camp.
And we went, the two of us illegally to-- through the mountains, walking the whole night, until we arrived to the Italian border, on some date.
And the Italian [? gendarmerie ?] was bribed, the police.
And that helped a lot, really, to go over the border.
No, no.
Everything was illegal.
But they knew about it.
I mean, you couldn't really go through the border and people not know about it.
But somehow it was hush-hushed.
We were going from Austria to Italy.
Again to a kibbutz, again to a kibbutz.
And when we arrived to Italy we went-- one of the very known and very rich Italian Jews gave away his holiday villa for our kibbutz.
Commander [INAUDIBLE].
And he gave us away his summer villa.
And where we actually married there, it was a little village out of Milano, 30 kilometer by train, called Albiate [NON-ENGLISH].
And in 1948, my parents arrived to-- to Italy from Austria.
And we got married.
Smilik was working at the time as a representative for a mizrahi in Milano.
And-- he was working very hard.
Because he was helping with the illegal immigration, and when we got married, he-- we didn't spend much of a honeymoon.
Because firstly I got appendicitis on the day of my wedding, and nearly went into hospital.
But somehow, seven days afterwards, I did went into hospital to have my operation, my appendix done.
And he wasn't with me, because he had to go to Genoa.
Because there was an illegal transport to be sent to Israel.
And everything was illegal.
So he used to send me cards.
I still got them from Genoa, or from Venice, or from Napoli.
And he couldn't say why he's not-- so he used to send me, because of that, I can't come home.
And on the folder was a storm or something.
And he used to work.
And I remember in hospital, the Italian women were so upset, it's such a young couple, and the husband is not with you.
And I could-- I couldn't explain to them why he is not with me.
Because what he was doing was illegal, and the Aliyah Bet used-- used to meet the transports of Jews who was running away from Poland, and from Austria, and from Czechoslovakia, and from Hungary.
And they used to go out in the middle of the night to meet him and help them, to-- to go over the station, from Italy to the vessels to take them to Israel.
No.
No, I wasn't working, no.
In Albiate, well only our kibbutz, about 180 people in our-- in our villa.
That was the [NON-ENGLISH], just our kibbutz, just 180 people.
And after that, he was transferred from Milano to Rome.
So I was still-- we were leaving-- after that, we went live in Milano.
We rented one room, which-- with an Italian family.
And he left to Rome to see whether we can settle there.
And I joined him later.
And we stayed in Rome for-- from 1949 to '54 we stayed in Rome.
Gilla was born in '51.
And I didn't have to work.
I was quite comfortable.
My family went to Israel, they was one of the first ones who went on-- on normal papers.
It wasn't Aliyah Bet anymore.
And my siblings-- my brother and my sister and my parents went to Israel.
And my father right away started working.
He was working in the Ministry of the Interior as also an accountant in the Department of-- print-- printing department till he died.
He had a very good job, and was very happy.
Who, we?
We came to Australia in 1950-- '52-- no, '54 sorry, '54.
That's a question you ask me.
We really shouldn't have come, because my father always dreamed of us-- for us to go to Israel to be united.
But somehow Smilik had a friend here, which was alarming us.
He was also left on his own from all the family.
And he wanted somebody to have with him.
So he's bombarding us with letters, come to Australia.
You know, if you work, you can earn good money.
And so and so, we sort of decided we're still so young.
We can go for a few years, make a bit of money, and eventually we immigrate to-- back to Israel.
But somehow it didn't happen.
Because then Gerry was born.
The school started, then university started, and so on and so forth.
Gerry was born in '57 here in Australia, in Sydney.
And somehow we didn't manage to fulfill our dream.
And sometimes I'm sorry that we didn't stay with our family.
And I suppose I'm still sorry.
After all, we've got a very comfortable life in Australia.
And Australia was good to us.
But it wasn't easy.
When I think about it, the first year I was-- for two or three years I was crying every night.
Why did I come here?
And-- but later on, it wasn't easy.
No.
Not-- we didn't ask.
And we started right away to work.
Smilik got a job and I would start manufacturing whatever I could.
I've never learned.
But somehow I always was to do something.
But it was until say about 35 years ago, we managed to get a shop, and-- and ever since, I was busy with the business.
I was quite successful.
I loved it.
And about a year ago, we retired.
And that's it.
I've got a nice family.
I've got four lovely grandchildren, which I adore.
The big one is Liza, which she's now at the moment in Israel having a great time.
And there is Talia.
That's the two children of Gilla, my daughter's children.
Liberman.
And then I've got Gerry.
And his wife is Linda.
And they've got two children.
One is Simon, which is five and a half; and Cara, which is eight and a half.
And that's all the family I've got here.
All my family lives in Israel.
OK.
